problem_summary: 2014 MCM Problem B ("discrete" type): build a model to find the best all-time college coach (male or female) across sports such as basketball, football, baseball, etc.; address whether the time-line horizon (coaching in 1913 vs 2013) matters; show the model generalizes across genders and all sports with a Top-5 list in 3 sports; and write a 1-2 page non-technical Sports Illustrated article. This team framed it as a multi-criteria evaluation/ranking problem and selected Pat Summitt as the overall best coach.
models_used: AHP-FCE Model I: Fuzzy Comprehensive Evaluation built on the Analytic Hierarchy Process. Coach metrics standardized to relative membership degrees, a judgment matrix derived from per-metric sample standard deviation, AHP weights, then composite score z(j)=sum_i w_i * r(i,j).

IAHP-FCE Model II (Improved): adds a 2-level classification hierarchy (first level = Experience / Achievement / Competency; second level = 13 metrics) to handle >9 metrics and simplify computation. Final score Z(j) aggregates per-classification fuzzy scores weighted by classification weights (0.35/0.40/0.25).

Data-driven judgment-matrix construction: b_ij computed from the ratio/spread of metric sample standard deviations s(i) (objective, data-driven rather than purely expert-elicited Saaty 1-9 entries), capped at a max scale of 9.

Single-objective linear programming for AHP consistency repair: minimize a Consistency Index Coefficient CIC(n) over a corrected reciprocal judgment matrix Y, subject to reciprocity, positivity, weights summing to 1, with tolerance band d in [0,0.5] (used d=0.2). Solved in LINGO (7.0/9.0); accept if CIC <= 0.1.

Metric standardization formulas: larger-is-better, smaller-is-better, and middle-is-best normalizations dividing by (x_max + x_min) to map raw stats to [0,1] relative membership degrees and remove dimensional effects.

Time-line horizon analysis: recompute each coach's yearly composite score over their career and plot composite-score-vs-year line charts (Dean Smith, Pat Summitt, Joe Paterno, Bobby Bowden) to argue era makes a bounded but slightly upward-trending difference.

Sensitivity analysis: perturb each metric's contribution by a percentage factor (1+r) in small +/-0.001 steps until the ranking order changes, recording the 'change threshold' interval per metric (MATLAB); average interval 0.0297 (<10%).
key_techniques: Deriving AHP judgment-matrix entries from the dispersion (sample standard deviation) of each standardized metric across coaches, so metrics that discriminate more between coaches get more weight — an objective alternative to subjective Saaty pairwise scoring.
Casting AHP consistency adjustment as an optimization (minimize CIC) solved with LINGO, giving a defensible, reproducible weight set rather than ad-hoc tweaking.

Two-stage model narrative: deliberately presenting a weaker baseline (Model I, ~80% Top-5 agreement, includes flagged failure of having 13 metrics > the 9-element AHP comfort limit) then an improved Model II (~100% Top-5 agreement) — turning a limitation into the motivation for the improvement.

Validation against external authority rankings (Yahoo/Bleacher Report 'most intriguing coaches' lists, official websites) as a ground-truth proxy, reporting set-overlap 'same rate' per sport (100% basketball/football/women's, 40% baseball).

Generalization-by-construction argument: same pipeline re-run on football (76 coaches), baseball (69), and women's basketball (15) just by swapping in each sport's metrics, demonstrating cross-sport/cross-gender transfer.
Three-tier symbols/assumptions scaffolding (coach / data / metrics assumptions) plus content-analysis + bibliometrics to justify the metric set selection.
why_it_won: From a judge's view it directly and completely answered every sub-prompt: a concrete model, an explicit time-line analysis with 4 case-study line charts, Top-5 lists in 3+ sports, demonstrated gender/sport generalization, AND a genuinely readable lay article. It validated quantitatively against independent expert rankings and reported a crisp accuracy story (80% -> ~100%), which reads as honest self-criticism plus measurable improvement — exactly the iterative modeling arc judges reward. The objective, data-driven weighting (standard-deviation-based judgment matrix) plus an optimization-based consistency check gave the otherwise-subjective AHP a rigorous, reproducible backbone. It closed with a full four-part model test (dimensional, deviation, assumption, sensitivity) and a memorable, counter-narrative headline result (a female coach, Pat Summitt, beating the men), which makes the paper stick with readers.
section_highlights: Strong structure: Summary + Abstract that already tell the whole 80%->100% arc and name the winner; explicit Restatement mapping each of the 4 prompt tasks; Literature Review weighing 5 candidate methods (peer review, survey, bibliometrics, AHP, FCE) before choosing AHP+FCE — shows method selection rather than method assumption. Clean assumptions split into coach/data/metrics buckets and a symbols table. The Model I -> 'Testing Conclusion' (admits inaccuracy, lists 3 concrete causes) -> Improving the model -> Model II flow is the paper's backbone and is very persuasive. Heavy use of hierarchy diagrams (Goal/Classification/Metrics trees per sport), per-sport weight tables, and validation comparison tables (14-17). The Sports Illustrated article genuinely re-explains the method in plain 'add up weighted scores' language with an inspirational coach-motivation closer. Each major claim is backed by a numbered citation.
pitfalls_or_limitations: English is broken throughout (grammar, typos, garbled coach names like 'Jim Calhou', 'Gordie Gillespie Augie / Garrido' merged across cells) — tolerated here but a real risk; clarity of the math carried it.

Several equations are visibly garbled/typeset-broken (e.g. formula (4) for b_ij, the CIC objective) and at least one consistency result is printed as 'min CIC(n) = < 0.1' with no value — reproducibility suffers.

Validation is circular-ish: 'accuracy' is overlap with popular media Top-25/Top-5 lists, not an objective ground truth; calling a re-ordered set (different rank order, same members) '100% correct' overstates agreement.

The headline winner depends on comparing raw composite scores ACROSS different sports/genders with different metric sets and different numbers of metrics — Pat Summitt's 0.883 (7 metrics, only 15 coaches) is not on the same scale as basketball's 0.71 (13 metrics, 39 coaches), so the cross-domain ranking is not apples-to-apples.

Classification-level weights (0.35/0.40/0.25 for Experience/Achievement/Competency) are asserted 'through access to relevant information' with no derivation — a subjective anchor undercutting the 'objective weighting' claim.
Small/uneven samples (baseball only 3 metrics, women's basketball only 15 coaches) force falling back to Model I and produce the weakest validation (baseball 40%).

Era/time-line conclusion is hand-wavy: 'bounded fluctuation with slight upward trend' from a few line charts, with no normalization for rule/era changes that the prompt explicitly asked about (1913 vs 2013).
Acknowledged missing data for intangibles (team-management experience, coaching skill), so the metric set is achievement/stat-heavy and omits qualitative coaching ability.
reusable_patterns: Standardize raw heterogeneous stats to [0,1] relative membership degrees (larger/smaller/middle-best variants) before any weighting — a clean, reusable preprocessing step for any MCM ranking/evaluation problem.

Build AHP judgment-matrix entries from each criterion's standard deviation across alternatives so discrimination drives weight — a transferable trick to make AHP objective and defendable when expert pairwise scores would feel arbitrary.

Wrap AHP consistency repair as an LP/optimization minimizing a consistency index (solvable in LINGO/Excel Solver) and report CIC<=0.1 as the pass criterion — reusable rigor for any weighted-criteria model.

Two-model 'baseline then improved' storytelling: ship a deliberately simpler Model I, quantify where it fails, then introduce a hierarchical Model II that fixes exactly those failure causes — a powerful, judge-pleasing narrative template.
Validate a subjective ranking against independent published expert lists and report a numeric agreement/overlap metric per category — a cheap, credible validation pattern when no ground truth exists.

Generalization-by-re-running: prove transfer to new sports/genders by swapping the metric set into the identical pipeline rather than building new models, and present Top-5 tables side-by-side with authority lists.

Four-pillar model-test section (dimensional consistency, model-vs-result deviation, assumption reasonableness, parameter sensitivity) plus a sensitivity-via-perturbation-until-rank-flips procedure — a reusable checklist to close out an MCM paper.

Mirror the technical pipeline in the required lay article with a single plain-language rule ('normalize, weight by importance, add up') and a thresholded branch (<10 metrics: flat weighting; >=10: classify into groups first).
